text: Hello friend, this is a case of bilateral ovarian dermoid. This patient has a six point five centimeter dermoid cyst on the right side and there is a one point eight mm dermoid cyst on the left side.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy, introducing a commercially available endo bag on the right side. The entire ovary is placed inside the endo bag to prevent contamination of the peritoneal cavity during aspiration or spillage.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to create a small puncture in the ovary, making it more vascular. A suction device is then inserted through the opening to aspirate the fluid content of the dermoid cyst, facilitating easier grasping with a surgical grasper.
A 5mm suction device is inserted into the dermoid cyst, extracting its fluid content. The cyst partially collapses, facilitating easier stripping with graspers.

The surgeon aspirates the fluid from the large dermoid cyst to prevent spontaneous rupture, ensuring controlled spillage. After suction, the inner cystic layer is held, allowing for the stripping of the outer ovarian cortex.

The surgeon uses ambidextrous technique to strip the outer ovarian cortex from the inner cystic layer, ensuring symmetrical enucleation from all sides to avoid undue force. The outer ovarian cortex is held with one hand while the other hand strips the tissue, facilitating the separation once a good plane is achieved.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector and a grasper to strip the outer ovarian cortex from the inner cystic layer of the dermoid cyst, taking advantage of the good plane for separation. The cyst is gradually being extracted, with the left hand holding and the right hand stripping. The dermoid cyst is easier to separate compared to the thicker-walled endometrioma.

Approximately fifty percent of the cyst is visible outside the ovary, with the remaining portion being separated. The fimbria is visible and protected, ensuring the fallopian tube is not stripped or touched. As the procedure progresses, seventy-five percent of the cyst is now out, leaving only twenty-five percent of the ovary still attached.

The surgeon completes the enucleation of the cyst, with approximately ninety percent of the cyst already separated and only ten percent remaining attached. A large hair ball is visible inside the cyst, which is held for final stripping. The cyst is then fully detached from the ovary and carefully placed into an endo bag, ensuring it reaches the base without further spillage by lifting the bag margins and applying jerky movements.

The surgeon places the dermoid cyst into the base of the endo bag, pulling the sleeve of the bag upwards to control spillage. The endo bag is then maneuvered towards the left side, allowing the left ovary to be dropped into the same bag.

The surgeon performs a left-sided ovarian cystectomy, cutting through the outer ovarian cortex on the left side. The procedure involves careful dissection to avoid rupture of the small 1.8 mm dermoid cyst, as confirmed by ultrasound.

The surgeon carefully dissects the base of the cyst, ensuring it remains intact to prevent rupture. The ovary is stripped out using a Grasper, while avoiding direct holding with the Maryland to prevent damage.

The surgeon confirms the cyst is intact and fully inside the endo bag. Using a 10 mm port, the mouth of the endo bag is extruded, and ovum forceps are used to remove the hair and rupture the cyst, extracting its contents one by one. A semi-traumatic grasper holds both sleeves of the endo bag, which is then inserted into the cannula for removal.

The surgeon places the cannula into the surgical field, and the mouth of the endobag is extradiated. Using long jaw artery forceps, the rectus is stretched, facilitating the removal of the hair ball from the cyst. The surgery concludes as the entire hair ball is extracted.
Of course you will not forget to close the fourth window. Because adenia is there if you do not close the fourth window. So thank you very much for watching this video.
